ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for seafood tom yum ban mian; closest defensible match is 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_03315595). Specific Thai-Singaporean fusion noodle soup not represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Noodle-based dishes) is the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for seafood tom yum ban mian in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared dish variants at this level of specificity.

main_ingredients: mixed seafood (prawns, squid, fish cake, clams)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of seafood tom yum ban mian soup is approximately 450g, comprising flat wheat noodles, mixed seafood, vegetables, and tom yum broth, as typically served at a Singapore hawker centre or food court.

nutrient_source.title: Seafood Tom Yum Ban Mian (Soup) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for 'Seafood tom yum ban mian (soup)', food code F05060113. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from this record. Saturated fat estimated at ~0.4g/100g based on HPB compositional data for similar seafood noodle soups; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, and other micronutrients not individually reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for seafood tom yum ban mian soup. GI estimated at 52 (low-medium boundary) based on analogous wheat noodle soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Flat wheat noodles (ban mian) are comparable to Chinese-style wheat noodles (GI ~47–55 per Sydney GI Database). The soup matrix, protein from seafood, and moderate fibre content further moderate the glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (38.2g for 450g bowl) / 100 = 19.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 420mg/100g; a 450g serving delivers approximately 1,890mg sodium, representing 82% of the 2,300mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl, avoid consuming the full broth, and request reduced tom yum paste and fish sauce to lower sodium intake below 400mg/100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 8.49g/100g with low dietary fibre (0.7g/100g); ban mian wheat noodles have a moderate GI (~52), and the low fibre content provides limited buffering for postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion with increased seafood and vegetables, increase the broth-to-noodle ratio, and monitor postprandial glucose response given the moderate glycaemic index of wheat noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 420mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; fat is low at 1.29g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~0.4g/100g, and seafood contributes beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit sodium exposure by avoiding the full broth and requesting reduced fish sauce; the low saturated fat content and omega-3 contribution from mixed seafood are cardioprotective elements that partially offset the sodium concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tom yum aromatics (lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves) and chilli contain polyphenols with anti-inflammatory properties; seafood contributes omega-3 fatty acids; saturated fat is very low at ~0.4g/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients; however, those with nightshade sensitivity should monitor for flares due to chilli padi content, and processed fish cake should be substituted with fresh seafood where possible to reduce pro-inflammatory additives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Tom Yum Ban Mian Soup is a Singaporean hawker dish that fuses Thai tom yum flavour with the local Chinese ban mian (flat hand-made wheat noodle) tradition. The soup base is prepared by simmering tom yum paste with lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, and chilli padi to produce a characteristically spicy, sour, and aromatic broth. Mixed seafood — typically prawns, squid, clams, and fish cake — is added and cooked briefly to retain tenderness. The flat wheat noodles are boiled separately and served in the broth with leafy vegetables and mushrooms. Per 100g, the dish provides 58 kcal, 3.19g protein, 1.29g fat, 8.49g carbohydrate, 0.7g dietary fibre, and 420mg sodium. The moderate glycaemic index (~52) reflects the wheat noodle base, while the soup matrix and seafood protein moderate the glycaemic response. Sodium is the primary clinical concern at 420mg/100g, making a full 450g bowl a high-sodium meal (~1,890mg). The dish is low in saturated fat and provides omega-3 fatty acids from seafood, which are cardioprotective. Tom yum aromatics contribute polyphenols and anti-inflammatory compounds. Suitable for general consumption in moderate portions; caution advised for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes patients due to sodium and carbohydrate load respectively.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F05060113) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from HPB compositional data for analogous seafood noodle soups. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous wheat noodle entries. All primary macros are exact HPB values.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F05060113 (Seafood tom yum ban mian soup) as of 2026. Limited published data for traditional preparation. Saturated fat estimated at 0.4g/100g based on HPB compositional data for comparable seafood noodle soup dishes; this is an approximation and should be confirmed if precise saturated fat data is required. Cultural classification corrected from input 'western' to 'Singaporean fusion (Thai-influenced)' — the dish is a Singapore hawker fusion of Thai tom yum flavour with Chinese ban mian noodles. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on wheat noodle soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in approved sources as of 2026. Halal status marked false as fish cake may contain non-halal additives; consumers should verify with the specific vendor.
